perturbation.original_text: Each party shall forthwith upon request by the other return to the other all Confidential Information (including, without limitation, all summaries, copies and excerpts of Confidential Information), and other information, given to it by the oth er and shall expunge and destroy all Confidential Information from any equipment or other device in its possession or control. Furthermore, the receiving party shall destroy all copies of analyses, compilations, studies or other documents or derivative wor ks which contain any Confidential Information. Each party shall on request provide a letter signed by a director confirming that it has complied with this clause.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall, upon request by the other, return to the other all Confidential Information given to it by the other. The receiving party shall destroy all copies of analyses, compilations, studies or other documents or derivative works which contain any Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The omission of the requirement to 'expunge and destroy' confidential information from devices and the removal of the confirmation letter requirement weakens the protection of confidential information. UK GDPR Article 17 requires data controllers to ensure erasure of personal data without undue delay. By removing the 'expunge and destroy' requirement, this conflicts with the 'right to be forgotten'.
perturbation.contradicted_law: UK GDPR Article 17 - Right to Erasure ('Right to be Forgotten')
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 17 of the UK GDPR grants individuals the right to have their personal data erased under specific circumstances. By removing the explicit requirement to 'expunge and destroy' the Confidential Information from any equipment or other device in its possession or control, this agreement risks non-compliance, particularly if the Confidential Information contains personal data covered by GDPR. The omission of the confirmation letter further hinders compliance, as it removes a verification step.
perturbation.justification: The original text contained a requirement to 'expunge and destroy' confidential information from devices, a clause removed in the changed text. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both state this omission creates a potential conflict with UK GDPR Article 17, which addresses the 'right to be forgotten,' particularly concerning the erasure of personal data. Although no scraped law snippets were provided to confirm the scope and applicability of UK GDPR Article 17 directly, the explanations provided indicate that removing the 'expunge and destroy' requirement weakens data protection and risks non-compliance, particularly if confidential information includes personal data. Removing the confirmation letter requirement also further weakens compliance. Thus, the removal of these requirements directly undermines the intent of data protection regulations. This represents a significant deviation from responsible data handling practices.

perturbation.original_text: 8.3. (Notwithstanding Clause 8.2) each party shall indemnify and keep indemnified the other party against any and all liabilities, claims, expenses, lo sses and damages suffered or incurred by that other party as a result of any breach by it of its obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.3. (Notwithstanding Clause 8.2) each party may consider indemnifying the other party against liabilities, claims, expenses, losses and damages suffered or incurred by that other party as a result of any breach by it of its obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall indemnify' to 'may consider indemnifying' removes the mandatory obligation for indemnification. This is a legal contradiction because under English law, particularly the Misrepresentation Act 1967, parties can be liable for misrepresentation, and indemnification clauses are crucial for allocating risk. By making it discretionary, it reduces the protection afforded to the non-breaching party and goes against standard legal practice.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Misrepresentation Act 1967 allows a party who has entered into a contract based on a misrepresentation to claim damages. Removing a mandatory indemnification clause impacts the remedies available to the claimant, and the strength of the agreement to provide suitable remedy for any impacts due to misrepresentation, going against principles established by the Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Misrepresentation Act 1967 | LawTeacher.net Loading Disclaimer: This work was produced by one of our expert legal writers, as a learning aid to help law students with their studies. Any information contained in this act summary does not constitute legal advice and should be treated as educational content only. Accept and close Share this: Facebook Twitter Reddit LinkedIn WhatsApp Brief Background The Misrepresentation Act 1967 is an Act which governs English contract law in the United Kingdom in situations where misrepresentation is involved, prior to parties entering into a contractual agreement. A misrepresentation can be considered to be a false statement of fact which prompts an individual to enter into a contract on the basis of that false statement. Misrepresentation can be established with three different types of statement. These are fraudulent, negligent and innocent misrepresentations and are often considered tiered in terms of severity by the courts. Why was it introduced? Misrepresentation is an important factor in both contract law and tort law in the English legal system. The Misrepresentation Act 1967 ensures that parties have an action to pursue in both contract law as well as the law of tort if the misrepresentation causes loss to one of the parties. Moreover, the Misrepresentation Act 1967 allows for the parties to be placed back into the same position that they would have been in before the contract was constructed as if a misrepresentation is found a contract can be effectively voided. This rule applies whether the misrepresentation was fraudulent, negligent or an innocent mistake. What was the aim of the Act? The introduction of the Misrepresentation Act 1967 was meant to provide a greater amount of security to parties that enter into a contractual agreement, to ensure that they are not tied to an
perturbation.justification: The original text mandated indemnification ('shall indemnify'), while the changed text makes it discretionary ('may consider indemnifying'). The Law Explanation states that removing a mandatory indemnification clause impacts the remedies available to the claimant. Scraped Law Snippet 2 highlights that the Misrepresentation Act 1967 ensures parties have an action to pursue if misrepresentation causes loss. By making indemnification discretionary, the changed text weakens the protection afforded to the non-breaching party, directly contradicting the intent of the Misrepresentation Act 1967 to provide security to parties entering into contractual agreements and ensuring remedies for misrepresentation, therefore this fits the Omission Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: 9.7. The parties agree that the construction, validity and performance of this Agreement shal l be governed by and construed under English Law, and for all matters arising under, out of, or in connection with the Agreement, they shall submit themselves to the exclusive jurisdiction of the English Courts.
perturbation.changed_text: 9.7. The parties agree that the construction, validity and performance of this Agreement shall be governed by and construed under English Law.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'they shall submit themselves to the exclusive jurisdiction of the English Courts' the agreement becomes ambiguous regarding dispute resolution. Under English law, specifically the Civil Jurisdiction and Judgments Act 1982 (and subsequent revisions), jurisdiction clauses provide certainty. Removing it could allow disputes to be litigated in multiple jurisdictions, potentially conflicting with established legal principles.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Civil Jurisdiction and Judgments Act 1982 addresses the allocation of jurisdiction in cross-border disputes. By removing the clause specifying exclusive jurisdiction to English Courts, the contract creates uncertainty and potential conflicts with this act, as it opens the door for disputes to be heard in other jurisdictions, potentially undermining the intended application of English Law.

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies the exclusive jurisdiction of English courts, while the changed text omits this, leading to ambiguity about where disputes will be resolved. This omission contradicts the purpose of jurisdiction clauses, which are designed to provide certainty and prevent conflicts, potentially undermining the application of English law as highlighted in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: 4.  EXCLUSIONS 4.1. The provisions of this Agreement shall not apply to information: 4.1.1. publicly available prior to the date of this Agreement or that is made publicly available on or after the date of this Agreement by the Disclosing Party without restriction; 4.1.2. that is independently obtained by the Receiving Party without breach by it of any obligation of confidentiality; 4.1.3. already in the Receiving Party's possession and was lawfully received from sources other than the Disclosing Party; 4.1.4. which is required to be disclosed by law, provided that, to the extent the Receiving Party is not prohibited from doing so by law, it undertakes to give the Disclosing Party not less than two business days’ notice of such requirement of disclosure and, to such extent, will mak e such disclosure in the manner and with the content agreed by the Disclosing Party. If any portion of Confidential Information falls within any of the above exceptions, the remainder shall continue to be subject to the restrictions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. EXCLUSIONS 4.1. The provisions of this Agreement shall not apply to information: 4.1.1. publicly available prior to the date of this Agreement or that is made publicly available on or after the date of this Agreement by the Disclosing Party without restriction; 4.1.2. that is independently obtained by the Receiving Party without breach by it of any obligation of confidentiality; 4.1.3. already in the Receiving Party's possession and was lawfully received from sources other than the Disclosing Party; 4.1.4. which is required to be disclosed by law. If any portion of Confidential Information falls within any of the above exceptions, the remainder shall continue to be subject to the restrictions of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause 'provided that, to the extent the Receiving Party is not prohibited from doing so by law, it undertakes to give the Disclosing Party not less than two business days’ notice of such requirement of disclosure and, to such extent, will mak e such disclosure in the manner and with the content agreed by the Disclosing Party' creates a potential conflict with whistleblowing legislation in the UK, such as the Public Interest Disclosure Act 1998. This act protects employees who disclose certain types of information, even if it's confidential.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Public Interest Disclosure Act 1998 protects whistleblowers who disclose information about wrongdoing. The original clause provided a mechanism for notice to the disclosing party before legally mandated disclosure. Removing this notice requirement means a Receiving Party could disclose confidential information under legal obligation (e.g., whistleblowing) without notifying the Disclosing Party, potentially facing legal action or hindering the whistleblowing process. This change could be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent whistleblower protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Whistleblowing for employees: What is a whistleblower - GOV.UK Cookies on GOV.UK We use some essential cookies to make this website work. We’d like to set additional cookies to understand how you use GOV.UK, remember your settings and improve government services. We also use cookies set by other sites to help us deliver content from their services. You have accepted additional cookies. You can change your cookie settings at any time. You have rejected additional cookies. You can change your cookie settings at any time. Accept additional cookies Reject additional cookies View cookies Hide this message Skip to main content Whistleblowing for employees Skip contents What is a whistleblower You’re a whistleblower if you’re a worker and you report certain types of wrongdoing. This will usually be something you’ve seen at work - though not always. The wrongdoing you disclose must be in the public interest. This means it must affect others, for example the general public. As a whistleblower you’re protected by law - you should not be treated unfairly or lose your job because you ‘blow the whistle’. You can raise your concern at any time about an incident that happened in the past, is happening now, or you believe will happen in the near future. Who is protected by law You’re protected if you’re a worker , for example you’re: an employee, such as a police officer, NHS employee, office worker, factory worker a trainee, such as a student nurse an agency worker a member of a Limited Liability Partnership (LLP) Get independent advice if you’re not sure you’re protected, for example from Citizens’ Advice. A confidentiality clause or ‘gagging clause’ in a settlement agreement is not valid if you’re a whistleblower. Complaints that count as whistleblowing You’re protected by law if you report any of the following: a criminal offence, for example fraud someone’s health and safety is in danger risk or actual damage to the environment a miscarriage of justice the company is breaking the law, for example does not have the right insurance you believe someone is covering up wrongdoing Complaints that do not count as whistleblowing Personal grievances (for example bullying, harassment, discrimination) are not covered by whistleblowing law, unless your particular case is in the public interest. Report these under your employer’s grievance policy . Contact the Advisory, Conciliation and Arbitration Service (Acas) for help and advice on resolving a workplace dispute. perturbation.justification: The original contract provided a process for disclosing confidential information required by law, including giving the Disclosing Party notice. By removing this notice requirement, the modified contract potentially contradicts whistleblower protection laws, specifically the Public Interest Disclosure Act 1998. The GOV.UK snippet defines a whistleblower and notes protections, stating, 'As a whistleblower you’re protected by law - you should not be treated unfairly or lose your job because you ‘blow the whistle’.' Removing the notice provision could hinder the whistleblowing process and potentially expose the Receiving Party to legal repercussions or discourage lawful disclosure, thus undermining the protection intended by whistleblowing legislation. The omission directly impacts the ability of the receiving party to comply with legal disclosure obligations while also adhering to the contract, thus creating a legal contradiction.
